content.text: Serverless computing lets you deploy and run code without provisioning or managing any servers — the cloud platform allocates execution environments on demand, scales them in parallel, and charges only for the compute time actually consumed. For agentic-AI systems, serverless is the natural home for the short-lived, event-driven pieces of the pipeline: webhook receivers, job-enqueue handlers, scheduled triggers, and lightweight data transformers. The long-running agent loop that calls an LLM dozens of times still belongs on a container, but serverless acts as the efficient, elastic front door.

Serverless is an umbrella term for two related compute models. Functions-as-a-Service (FaaS) — AWS Lambda, Google Cloud Functions, Azure Functions — lets you deploy a single handler function (Python, Node, Go, Java) that the platform invokes in response to an event. The function runs inside an ephemeral container managed entirely by the provider; you supply only the handler code and its dependencies. Serverless containers — Google Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps, AWS App Runner — extend this to Docker images: the platform scales container replicas from zero to many and back again, billing per CPU-second used.

The unifying property across both variants is the scale-to-zero default: when no requests are arriving, no compute resources are allocated and no charges accrue. The platform provisions new instances automatically when demand appears. This contrasts sharply with traditional always-on VMs or bare-metal servers, where idle capacity still costs money and human operators must plan capacity in advance.

In the context of agentic AI, the relevant platforms include AWS Lambda (up to 10 GB RAM, 15-minute timeout, container-image support), Google Cloud Functions 2nd-gen (up to 32 GB RAM, 60-minute timeout), Azure Functions (Flex Consumption plan, up to 4 GB), Google Cloud Run (up to 32 GB, 60-minute request timeout, ideal for SSE/streaming), and AWS Fargate with scale-to-zero (minutes-long tasks, no hard timeout). Each has its own event-source integrations, billing granularity, and cold-start characteristics.

Agentic workloads are inherently bursty. A product might serve zero agent runs at 3 a.m. and ten thousand concurrent runs during a live demo or viral event. Provisioning EC2 instances or ECS services for the peak load means paying for idle capacity during off-hours. Serverless eliminates that waste: you pay only when code executes, and the platform handles the burst automatically without manual scaling decisions.

Beyond cost, serverless reduces operational surface area. Running an agent pipeline on VMs means maintaining OS images, security patches, monitoring agents, auto-scaling groups, load balancers, and cluster lifecycle scripts. A Lambda function, by contrast, requires none of that — you write a handler, push a deployment package, and the platform takes care of everything else. For small teams building and iterating on agent systems, this reduction in ops work is often the deciding factor.

Serverless also integrates natively with the event-driven components that agentic pipelines rely on. AWS Lambda can be triggered directly by SQS, SNS, EventBridge, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, and API Gateway without any polling code. Google Cloud Functions integrates with Pub/Sub and Cloud Storage events. These native integrations eliminate the infrastructure glue code that would otherwise be needed to connect an event source to a processing function.
— no OS patching, AMI updates, cluster node lifecycles, or capacity planning. The platform owns the compute layer entirely.
— idle agent systems cost nothing at rest. Scale-out happens in seconds on traffic spikes without manual intervention.
— Lambda charges per 1ms of compute time multiplied by memory allocation, plus $0.20 per million requests. At low-to-medium volume, this is significantly cheaper than always-on containers.
— SQS, EventBridge, Pub/Sub, API Gateway, Cloud Storage, and dozens more wire to serverless functions without polling loops.
— a Lambda function update deploys and takes effect in under 10 seconds. No container image rebuilds or cluster rolling updates required.
— the platform distributes execution across multiple availability zones automatically. There is no single point of failure to design around.
— each invocation runs in its own isolated environment. One slow or failing invocation does not block others; the platform spawns additional instances transparently.

— AWS Lambda caps at 15 minutes. Google Cloud Functions 2nd-gen allows 60 minutes. A multi-turn agent loop that calls a slow LLM can easily exceed these limits, making FaaS unsuitable for the worker tier.

— when no warm instance exists, the platform must initialise a container, load the runtime, and import dependencies before the handler runs. Python Lambda cold starts range from 200ms to 2+ seconds depending on package size; this is unacceptable for latency-sensitive synchronous APIs.

— each invocation starts with a clean, ephemeral environment. Any in-memory state from a previous call is gone. Agent conversation history and run status must live in an external store (DynamoDB, Redis, S3).

— traditional breakpoints do not work in a cloud-invoked function. Distributed tracing (AWS X-Ray, Cloud Trace, OpenTelemetry) and structured logging are required to diagnose problems across hundreds of concurrent invocations.
— the trigger wiring (SQS ARNs, EventBridge rules, Pub/Sub subscriptions) is cloud-provider-specific and tightly couples the function to one platform. Moving providers requires rewiring all triggers.

— Lambda's default account concurrency limit is 1000 (adjustable). At high burst, concurrent invocations can exhaust downstream LLM API rate limits, causing cascading throttle errors. Reserved concurrency per function is essential.
— API Gateway synchronous responses are limited to 10 MB; SQS message bodies to 256 KB. Large agent outputs must be stored in S3 and referenced by pointer, not returned inline.

Serverless functions support two invocation modes. Synchronous invocation (used by API Gateway, Lambda function URLs, Cloud Run) blocks the caller until the function returns a response. The calling client holds an open HTTP connection for the duration. Asynchronous invocation (used by SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Pub/Sub) decouples the caller from the function: the event source delivers the message to an internal queue, and the platform invokes the function independently. The caller receives a 202 acknowledgement immediately. For agent pipelines, synchronous invocation is appropriate for the lightweight enqueue endpoint (fast, sub-second), while asynchronous invocation drives the worker tier.

When a function is invoked, the platform first checks for a warm (already-running) execution environment. If one is available, it reuses it — the handler runs immediately and global-scope objects (database connections, loaded models, SDK clients) persist across calls within that instance. If no warm instance exists, the platform executes a cold start: it downloads the deployment package, initialises the container, starts the language runtime, runs the module-level initialisation code, and then invokes the handler.

Cold start duration depends on: runtime choice (Python and Node.js are faster than Java or .NET), deployment package size (smaller is faster), memory allocation (higher memory = more CPU = faster initialisation), and VPC attachment (adding VPC networking adds ~100ms for ENI setup). Keeping packages lean — under 10 MB for pure-Python functions, using Lambda Layers for shared dependencies — is the primary lever for reducing cold start latency.

Lambda charges on two dimensions: request count ($0.20 per million) and compute duration (per GB-second). A GB-second is 1 second of execution with 1 GB of memory allocated; duration is rounded up to the nearest 1 ms.

For 500,000 invocations, each running for 300 ms at 512 MB, the compute cost is roughly: 500,000 × 0.3 × 0.5 × $0.0000166667 ≈ $1.25, plus $0.10 for requests — a total of about $1.35. The same workload on an always-on t3.medium ($0.0416/h) would cost $30/month even at zero traffic.

The recommended architecture for serverless agentic AI separates concerns into three tiers: a thin, fast serverless ingress layer; a durable message queue; and long-running container workers. The diagram below illustrates how HTTP requests, cron jobs, and async callbacks flow through the system.

attrs.alt: Serverless Agent Architecture: Client → API Gateway → Lambda (enqueue) → SQS Queue → Container Worker (ECS Fargate / Cloud Run) → LLM API and State Store, with EventBridge cron path and async callback webhook

content.text: The ingress Lambda functions are intentionally minimal: validate the request, write a row to the state store, publish a message to the queue, and return HTTP 202. All LLM execution happens in the container worker tier, which polls the queue and runs the full agent loop without time pressure. This design means Lambda's 15-minute timeout is irrelevant to the actual agent work, and the queue provides natural backpressure — if workers are busy, jobs wait in the queue rather than being dropped.
Receiving webhooks from external systems (GitHub, Stripe, Twilio) and enqueuing jobs.
Scheduling nightly evaluation runs, data ingestion, or cleanup tasks via cron.
Running lightweight API validation, token counting, or routing logic that completes in under 5 minutes.
Building event-driven transformations on data streams (DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, Pub/Sub) where each record takes milliseconds to process.
Prototype or early-stage products where the ops burden of managing containers is not yet justified by traffic volume.
The workload is too complex for a zip-deployed function but traffic is intermittent enough that always-on containers are wasteful.
The agent serves streaming HTTP responses (SSE, WebSocket upgrades) that must persist for minutes.
The team wants container portability (Docker image) without paying for idle ECS tasks.
Timeouts of up to 60 minutes are sufficient (Cloud Run) and no persistent SQS polling is needed.
Running a full multi-turn agent loop that may take 15+ minutes per invocation — use ECS Fargate or a long-running Kubernetes pod instead.
Maintaining persistent WebSocket connections to many concurrent clients; Lambda's connection model is per-invocation, not long-lived.
Processing high-throughput, steady-state workloads at millions of requests per hour where always-on containers are cheaper per unit of compute.
Loading large ML models into memory on every cold start; the initialisation overhead makes FaaS impractical for local inference.
Step 1: Write the Lambda enqueue handler
The function validates the incoming request, writes an initial record to DynamoDB, and publishes a message to SQS. It must complete in well under a second to avoid holding the caller's connection.

import json
import os
import hmac
import hashlib
import uuid
import boto3

sqs = boto3.client("sqs")
dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["STATE_TABLE"])
QUEUE_URL = os.environ["AGENT_QUEUE_URL"]
WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ["WEBHOOK_SECRET"]


def handler(event, context):
    # Verify HMAC signature from calling service
    body = event.get("body") or ""
    sig = (event.get("headers") or {}).get("x-signature", "")
    expected = hmac.new(WEBHOOK_SECRET.encode(), body.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(expected, sig):
        return {"statusCode": 401, "body": json.dumps({"error": "invalid signature"})}

    payload = json.loads(body)
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())

    # Persist initial run state
    dynamo.put_item(Item={"run_id": run_id, "status": "queued", "prompt": payload["prompt"]})

    # Enqueue for async processing
    sqs.send_message(
        QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
        MessageBody=json.dumps({
            "run_id": run_id,
            "prompt": payload["prompt"],
            "webhook_url": payload.get("webhook_url"),
        }),
    )

    return {
        "statusCode": 202,
        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        "body": json.dumps({"run_id": run_id, "status": "queued"}),
    }
Step 2: Configure SQS queue with a Dead-Letter Queue

# AWS SAM template snippet
Resources:
  AgentQueue:
    Type: AWS::SQS::Queue
    Properties:
      QueueName: agent-jobs
      VisibilityTimeout: 3600      # must be > worker processing time
      RedrivePolicy:
        deadLetterTargetArn: !GetAtt AgentDLQ.Arn
        maxReceiveCount: 3         # move to DLQ after 3 failed attempts

  AgentDLQ:
    Type: AWS::SQS::Queue
    Properties:
      QueueName: agent-jobs-dlq
      MessageRetentionPeriod: 1209600  # 14 days

  EnqueueFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: handler.handler
      Runtime: python3.12
      MemorySize: 256
      Timeout: 10
      Environment:
        Variables:
          AGENT_QUEUE_URL: !Ref AgentQueue
          STATE_TABLE: !Ref StateTable
          WEBHOOK_SECRET: !Sub "{{resolve:secretsmanager:webhook-secret}}"
      Events:
        Api:
          Type: HttpApi
          Properties:
            Path: /run
            Method: POST
The worker runs as a long-lived process on ECS Fargate or Cloud Run. It polls the queue, processes jobs one at a time, and deletes each message only after successful completion.

import asyncio
import json
import os
import time
import boto3
import anthropic
import requests

sqs = boto3.client("sqs")
dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["STATE_TABLE"])
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
QUEUE_URL = os.environ["AGENT_QUEUE_URL"]
MAX_TURNS = 10


def update_status(run_id: str, status: str, result: str | None = None):
    expr = "SET #s = :s"
    names = {"#s": "status"}
    values = {":s": status}
    if result is not None:
        expr += ", #r = :r"
        names["#r"] = "result"
        values[":r"] = result
    dynamo.update_item(
        Key={"run_id": run_id},
        UpdateExpression=expr,
        ExpressionAttributeNames=names,
        ExpressionAttributeValues=values,
    )


def run_agent(run_id: str, prompt: str, webhook_url: str | None) -> None:
    update_status(run_id, "running")
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]

    for _ in range(MAX_TURNS):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            messages=messages,
        )
        reply = resp.content[0].text
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": reply})
        if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            break

    final = messages[-1]["content"]
    update_status(run_id, "done", final)

    if webhook_url:
        try:
            requests.post(webhook_url, json={"run_id": run_id, "result": final}, timeout=15)
        except Exception as exc:
            print(f"webhook delivery failed run_id={run_id} error={exc}")


def poll_loop() -> None:
    print("Worker started, polling queue...")
    while True:
        resp = sqs.receive_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            MaxNumberOfMessages=1,
            WaitTimeSeconds=20,   # long polling — reduces empty receives
        )
        for msg in resp.get("Messages", []):
            body = json.loads(msg["Body"])
            try:
                run_agent(body["run_id"], body["prompt"], body.get("webhook_url"))
            except Exception as exc:
                print(f"agent error run_id={body['run_id']} error={exc}")
                update_status(body["run_id"], "error")
                raise  # let SQS retry via visibility timeout
            sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL, ReceiptHandle=msg["ReceiptHandle"])


if __name__ == "__main__":
    poll_loop()
Example 1: Google Cloud Function (Node.js) triggered by Pub/Sub
This function receives a base64-encoded Pub/Sub message containing the agent job description and publishes it to a downstream topic for the worker to consume.
Example 2: EventBridge-triggered Lambda for nightly evaluation
A scheduled Lambda runs nightly to kick off an automated evaluation suite, creating one SQS message per test case. The worker processes evaluations asynchronously.

# eval_trigger.py — AWS Lambda triggered by EventBridge rule (rate: 1 day)
import json
import os
import uuid
import boto3

sqs = boto3.client("sqs")
s3 = boto3.client("s3")

QUEUE_URL = os.environ["AGENT_QUEUE_URL"]
EVAL_BUCKET = os.environ["EVAL_BUCKET"]
EVAL_KEY = "evals/nightly_suite.json"


def handler(event, context):
    # Load the evaluation test suite from S3
    obj = s3.get_object(Bucket=EVAL_BUCKET, Key=EVAL_KEY)
    test_cases = json.loads(obj["Body"].read())

    entries = []
    for tc in test_cases:
        run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
        sqs.send_message(
            QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
            MessageBody=json.dumps({
                "run_id": run_id,
                "prompt": tc["prompt"],
                "expected": tc.get("expected"),
                "eval_mode": True,
            }),
        )
        entries.append({"run_id": run_id, "case_id": tc["id"]})

    print(json.dumps({"event": "eval_batch_queued", "count": len(entries)}))
    return {"statusCode": 200, "body": json.dumps({"queued": len(entries)})}

A B2B SaaS company ships an AI documentation assistant that processes customer support ticket attachments. The pipeline handles a steady baseline of around 500 jobs per day, with bursts to 15,000 jobs during large customer onboarding events — a 30x spike in less than an hour.

The architecture: an HTTPS API endpoint on API Gateway triggers a Lambda function (512 MB, 10-second timeout) that validates the JWT, writes a row to DynamoDB, and sends a message to SQS. An ECS Fargate service running three to twenty tasks (scaling on ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible) polls the queue. Each task runs the agent loop: it calls the Anthropic API up to 8 turns, extracts structured data, and writes the result back to DynamoDB. A final Lambda, triggered by a DynamoDB Stream on completed rows, fires the customer's registered webhook with the extracted data.

The enqueue Lambda never touches the LLM — it completes in under 100 ms. The Fargate tasks run jobs averaging 45 seconds each, well within the per-task hourly billing window. During the 30x spike, Fargate scales from 3 to 18 tasks in 4 minutes, draining the queue backlog entirely within 25 minutes. The total burst cost for that event: approximately $2.40 in Lambda compute, $0.08 in SQS, and $18 in Fargate task time.
Running the full LLM call synchronously in Lambda

The most common mistake is placing the entire agent loop inside a Lambda handler. A single GPT-4 call can take 30–120 seconds; a multi-turn loop easily exceeds Lambda's 15-minute ceiling. Worse, the Lambda holds an open HTTP connection to the caller for the entire duration, which API Gateway's own 29-second integration timeout will kill first.

# WRONG: entire LLM call inside Lambda
def handler(event, context):
    prompt = json.loads(event["body"])["prompt"]
    # This can take 2-30 minutes for multi-turn agents.
    # Lambda will kill it at 15 min; API GW at 29 seconds.
    result = anthropic.Anthropic().messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=8192,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return {"statusCode": 200, "body": result.content[0].text}
Storing conversation state in a module-level dict

Because Lambda reuses warm execution environments across invocations, developers sometimes store state in a global dictionary and observe it persist between calls within the same instance. This works inconsistently: parallel invocations get separate environments, cold starts wipe the dict, and updates from one instance are invisible to others.

# CORRECT: external state store accessed on every invocation
import boto3
dynamo = boto3.resource("dynamodb").Table(os.environ["SESSIONS_TABLE"])

def handler(event, context):
    sid = event["session_id"]
    item = dynamo.get_item(Key={"session_id": sid}).get("Item", {"messages": []})
    item["messages"].append(event["message"])
    dynamo.put_item(Item={"session_id": sid, **item})
    return {"messages": item["messages"]}
Oversized deployment packages causing slow cold starts

Installing full data-science stacks (pandas, numpy, scipy, scikit-learn) into a Lambda package can push the zipped size to 50–100 MB, turning every cold start into a 5–10 second delay. The fix is to use slim dependencies: httpx instead of requests where possible, individual boto3 service clients (already available in the Lambda runtime) instead of bundling the full SDK, and Lambda Layers for shared heavy dependencies.
— Lambda/Cloud Function as the ingress gate (validate, enqueue, return 202), container worker for execution. Never put long LLM calls in FaaS.
— SQS and Pub/Sub guarantee at-least-once delivery. Design the handler so that processing the same message twice produces the same result. Use the
as a deduplication key in DynamoDB: check if a result already exists before doing work.

— configure a maximum concurrent execution count on each Lambda to prevent one function from consuming the entire account limit and starving others. For enqueue functions that call DynamoDB, a concurrency of 100–200 is typically sufficient.

— any asynchronous invocation (SQS, EventBridge) should route failed messages to a DLQ after N retries. Monitor DLQ depth as an alarm metric; unprocessed DLQ messages indicate bugs or infrastructure failures.
Use structured logging with correlation IDs
, duration, and error fields. This makes CloudWatch Logs Insights or Cloud Logging queries trivially filterable.
Tune memory for performance, not just cost

— Lambda allocates CPU proportional to memory. A function running in 512 MB that takes 800 ms might run in 300 ms at 1024 MB; the higher memory cost is offset by the shorter duration, and the latency improvement is real.
Use provisioned concurrency for latency-critical paths
— the auth/token-validation Lambda that users hit synchronously should have 2–5 pre-warmed instances via provisioned concurrency to eliminate cold-start latency on first request of the day.
— target under 5 MB zipped for FaaS functions. Use Lambda Layers for shared ML libraries. Delete test files and documentation from packages before zipping.

Cold start latency has three components: container download (minimised by smaller packages and Lambda's caching), runtime initialisation (minimised by choosing Python or Node over Java), and user code initialisation (minimised by deferring expensive imports). Moving SDK client construction and environment variable reads to module scope (outside the handler function) means they execute once on cold start and are reused on warm calls. This pattern — called the "warm client" pattern — reduces per-invocation overhead significantly.

For production workloads where even occasional cold starts are unacceptable, AWS Lambda Provisioned Concurrency pre-warms N instances and keeps them warm indefinitely. Billed at a slightly higher rate than on-demand, it eliminates cold starts for that function entirely. Google Cloud Run's minimum-instances setting serves the same purpose for containerised functions.
Concurrency limits and queue depth scaling

Lambda's default account concurrency limit is 1,000 concurrent executions across all functions. An enqueue spike of 10,000 simultaneous requests against an API Gateway/Lambda stack will only process 1,000 of them in parallel; the rest are throttled with HTTP 429. The solution is to size reserved concurrency per function based on expected peak load, and to ensure downstream systems (DynamoDB write capacity, SQS send rate) can handle the concurrency.
For the worker tier, scale based on queue depth rather than CPU utilisation. An ECS service scaling policy on
that targets 10 messages per task creates proportional, fast response to backlogs. Set a scale-in cooldown of 120 seconds to avoid thrashing when the queue drains.

Set Lambda memory to the minimum that keeps execution within acceptable latency bounds. Use AWS Lambda Power Tuning (an open-source step functions state machine) to benchmark 128 MB through 3 GB in a single automated run — it identifies the memory setting with the lowest cost-per-invocation. For agent enqueue functions that do only I/O (DynamoDB write + SQS send), 256 MB is usually sufficient. Set the timeout to 3× the p99 observed execution time, not the maximum of 15 minutes, so runaway executions are terminated promptly.
— CloudFormation extension with shorthand for Lambda, API Gateway, and DynamoDB.
packages and deploys in one command.
runs functions locally against mocked events.
— constructs library for defining Lambda, SQS, EventBridge, and IAM roles as typed Python or TypeScript. Better for complex multi-resource stacks than SAM YAML.
— provider-agnostic YAML-based deployment tool. Supports AWS, GCP, and Azure from one config file. Less tightly coupled to CloudFormation than SAM.
— AWS-specific Python microframework that generates SAM templates from Flask-like route decorators. Rapid prototype for Lambda REST APIs.
— open-source Step Functions state machine that tests a Lambda at multiple memory sizes and plots cost vs performance. Run it before setting production memory.
— local server that emulates Cloud Functions triggers (HTTP, Pub/Sub, CloudEvent). Run
— Cloud Run services serve HTTP; Cloud Run jobs are batch/cron. Both deploy from a Docker image with
— local emulator for Azure Functions. Supports Durable Functions orchestrations, which provide stateful long-running workflows as chains of short-lived functions.

# Serverless Framework: deploy to AWS from serverless.yml
npm install -g serverless
serverless deploy --stage prod

# AWS SAM: build and deploy
sam build
sam deploy --guided

# Google Cloud Functions: deploy from source directory
gcloud functions deploy agentEnqueue \
  --gen2 \
  --runtime=nodejs20 \
  --trigger-topic=inbound-webhooks \
  --entry-point=agentEnqueue \
  --memory=256MB \
  --timeout=60s \
  --region=us-central1
Choosing the right compute tier for each component of an agentic pipeline depends on execution duration, traffic shape, startup latency tolerance, and operational overhead tolerance.

A common production pattern combines multiple rows: Lambda or Cloud Run for the HTTP ingress layer, SQS or Pub/Sub as the decoupling queue, and ECS Fargate (with min 0, max 20 tasks) for the agent worker pool. This gives the cost profile of serverless at rest and the unlimited execution time of containers under load.
Every Lambda function executes under an IAM role that should grant only the permissions the function actually needs. An enqueue function needs
on one specific queue ARN and
on one specific table ARN — nothing more. Avoid attaching AWS-managed policies like
which grant write access to all tables in the account. Use the CDK or SAM

Never store API keys in Lambda environment variables in plain text — they appear in the Lambda console and CloudTrail logs. Use AWS Secrets Manager or Parameter Store (SecureString) and fetch the secret at warm-start time (once, in module scope). For the Anthropic API key specifically:

import boto3
import json
import os

# Fetch at module scope: runs once on cold start, reused on warm invocations
def _load_secret(name: str) -> str:
    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name=os.environ["AWS_REGION"])
    return json.loads(client.get_secret_value(SecretId=name)["SecretString"])["api_key"]

ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = _load_secret(os.environ["ANTHROPIC_SECRET_NAME"])

SQS standard queues deliver messages at least once, not exactly once. A network glitch between the worker deleting the SQS message and DynamoDB confirming the result can cause the same job to be processed twice. Guard against this with a conditional write: before running the agent, attempt to write
to DynamoDB with a condition that the item must not already exist. If the condition fails, the job was already processed — skip it and delete the SQS message.

Set a VisibilityTimeout on the SQS queue that exceeds the maximum expected worker processing time. If a worker crashes mid-job, the message becomes visible again after the timeout and another worker picks it up. Set it to the p99.9 worker duration plus a safety margin — for most agent pipelines, 30 to 60 minutes.

for the DLQ, triggering at one message. Any message reaching the DLQ represents a job that failed three consecutive times — this warrants immediate investigation. Route DLQ alarm notifications to PagerDuty or Slack via SNS. Never silently discard DLQ messages; retain them for at least 14 days to allow root-cause analysis and reprocessing.
Serverless FaaS (Lambda, Cloud Functions) eliminates server management and scales to zero, making it cost-effective for bursty, intermittent agentic workloads.
Use the front-door pattern: serverless ingress (validate + enqueue) → durable message queue → container worker. FaaS handles the fast gate; containers handle the long LLM execution.
Cold starts are real — keep deployment packages under 5 MB, use module-scope client construction, and enable provisioned concurrency for latency-critical synchronous paths.
Serverless functions are stateless by design; all conversation history, run status, and intermediate results must live in an external store such as DynamoDB or Redis.
Set a Dead-Letter Queue on every async trigger and alarm on DLQ depth. At-least-once delivery means idempotent handlers with deduplication logic are required for correctness.
Reserved concurrency limits on each Lambda function prevent one function from exhausting the account limit and protect downstream rate-limited services like the LLM API.

Serverless containers (Cloud Run, Azure Container Apps) bridge the gap between 15-minute FaaS limits and always-on containers — deploy Docker images that scale to zero with up to 60-minute request timeouts.
